schools.summary: Matthew Henry dwells on the ark's homecoming — its circumstances better than before, yet short of the temple; David's steadfast tent outdoing Solomon's forsaken temple, so 'the church's poorest times were its purest.'

Ellicott: the first three verses are wrongly split from ch. 15; the narrative parallels 2 Samuel 6, but the Chronicler is careful to show the priests and Levites, not David alone, ministering in the sacrifices.
Clarke gives a bird's-eye outline of the whole chapter — ark, sacrifices, portions, ministers, thanksgiving, daily service, dismissal.
Gill passes over the verses as matter already told in the parallel account.

themes: the ark lodged in its tent

schools.summary: Ellicott reads 'the burnt offering' as one great holocaust, notes the verse is all but identical with 2 Sam. 6:18 (omitting 'Sabaoth'), and sets the blessing beside Num. 6:22-27; Keil sees David here setting the Levites' service in order, glossing 'to record' as 'to bring into remembrance.'
Henry: the blessing is the king's act as father and prophet, and (with the Targum) 'in the name of the Word of the Lord' — the eternal Word who is Jehovah.

themes: the blessing in the name of the LORD

schools.summary: Clarke: a whole cake and, with the Targum, 'the sixth part of an ox and of a hin of wine' (cf. 2 Sam. 6:18-20, Jarchi).

Ellicott parses each term — kikkar (round cake), the rare word for a portion of the peace-offerings, and 'flagon of wine' better read as a raisin-cake (Hosea 3:1; Isaiah 16:7); the gift reaches every man and woman.
Henry: the royal treat shows David generous to his subjects as he had found God gracious to him; enlarged hearts should be open-handed.

Ellicott unfolds the three verbs — record, thank, praise — 'to record' being the technical chant accompanying the Azkarah (Lev. 2:2; cf. the titles of Ps. 38, 70), 'to praise' the hallelujah of Ps. 146-150; Keil sets the appointment at the close of the festival, David ordering the Levites' song on Zion.
Gill: the Levites were appointed to minister before the ark by singing God's praises.
Wesley: all our rejoicings should express themselves in thanksgivings to him from whom all our comforts are received.
Keil and Delitzsch read out the whole company and its instruments and press the phrase 'at the first,' marking the very moment liturgical singing was introduced into Israel's worship.
Gill: Asaph is 'chief' of this choir, though of the three principal singers Heman was chief and Asaph next.
Clarke refers the reader back to the muster of the Levites in the preceding chapter (1 Chr. 15:17).

Ellicott: Jahaziel is unmatched in ch. 15 (unless the later Eliezer); the musicians number twelve, answering to the twelve tribes; the 'trumpets' are straight clarions and 'continually' means service at fixed recurring times.
Gill notes only that Benaiah and Jahaziel the priests were appointed to blow the trumpets.
Henry: David by the Spirit composed the thanksgiving and delivered it to Asaph; though 'gathered out of several psalms,' it warrants our framing hymns from David's psalms.
Clarke: David made the psalm on this occasion and gave it to Asaph to sing; Wesley notes 'first' implies later psalms followed, and finds the words in Ps. 105:1-15 and Ps. 96:1-11.

Ellicott presses four reasons that the ode is a later insertion — a visibly-seamed cento, the anonymous source-psalms, no express claim of Davidic authorship, and no allusion to the occasion — an editor supplying the gap from the Psalter.
Gill, marking only the day and the ministers appointed, assumes the plain Davidic sense.

debates.question: Did David compose this psalm, or is it a later compilation inserted into the Chronicles?

sides.position: David himself composed it by the Spirit and delivered it to Asaph at the ark's entry
a later editor strung together three existing psalms as a mosaic and inserted the ode, its language betraying a post-Davidic origin

themes: David's Spirit-led artistry versus a later compiler

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch read the whole ode's architecture out of this opening verse; Ellicott glosses 'give thanks' (Ps. 105 as a todah), 'call upon his name,' 'make known' as Israel's mission, and 'the people' as 'the peoples.'
Henry: glorify God by thanksgivings, prayers, and discourse, and make known his deeds that strangers may be led to adore him.

themes: the architecture of the whole ode

schools.summary: Ellicott counts three words for one act of praise — sing, sing psalms (with accompaniment), chant — upon 'his wondrous works,' things separate and out of the common (Exod. 3:20); Keil carries the summons on as one unbroken call to seek God's presence.
Henry: we glorify God by our songs and our discourse, talking of all his wondrous works.
Ellicott traces 'seek the LORD' through the Chronicler's own vocabulary (1 Chr. 13:3; 15:13) and notes the doubled call to seek across this verse and the next.
Henry: those who give glory to God's name are allowed to glory in it; let the heart rejoice that seeks the Lord, and much more that has found him.

themes: the joy of those who seek

schools.summary: Ellicott reads 'the LORD and his strength' against Exod. 15:2 and Isaiah 26:4, takes 'his face' as his presence in the sanctuary, and coins the maxim 'true devotion is the secret of moral strength.'
Henry: to seek the Lord and his strength is to seek him 'by the ark of his strength,' and to seek his face continually is to make it the constant posture of the soul.

themes: seek the LORD and his strength

schools.summary: Ellicott opens the second strophe of Ps. 105 and sorts the three objects of remembrance — marvellous works, portents, and 'the judgments of his mouth,' self-executing utterances like the word of Gen. 1:3 and Exod. 12:12.
Clarke: the whole psalm refers to God's wondrous actions among the nations on Israel's behalf.

Ellicott: 'seed of Israel' (for the psalm's 'Abraham,' Ps. 105:6) improves the parallelism and is probably a correction; 'his servant' is plural in the LXX, recalling Israel as the servant of Jehovah in Isaiah. Keil takes the vocative up into the running summons.
Ellicott: 'the grand thought of Israel that, though Jehovah is their God, he is not theirs exclusively — he governs the wide world.'
Henry glorifies God at once as ruler of the whole creation, whose judgments are in all the earth, and as ours — 'He is the Lord our God.'

themes: He is the LORD our God
his judgments in all the earth

schools.summary: Ellicott: the psalm's 'He hath remembered' (Ps. 105:8) is here turned into an exhortation, 'Be ye mindful'; 'the word which he commanded' he renders 'the promise which he established' for a thousand generations.
Henry: seeing God will be ever mindful of his covenant, we never must forget it; it is 'commanded' because he has authority to promise and ability to perform.

themes: a promise for a thousand generations

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'even of the covenant' should be cancelled (the object is still the promise); 'made' is literally 'cut' (Haggai 2:5), the oath to Abraham of Gen. 22:16; and Isaac stands in a fuller form than Ps. 105:9.
Henry: the covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, long dead, is still sure to the spiritual seed and its promises pleadable.

themes: still sure to the spiritual seed

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'hath confirmed' is future in Ps. 105:10; 'the same' is still the promise; 'for a law' means a fixed decree, and 'everlasting covenant' sets no term.
Henry: the covenant is the great matter of joy because it is everlasting — a decree that cannot be repealed, sure to the spiritual seed.

themes: confirmed to Jacob for a law
the keystone of the covenant strophe

schools.summary: Ellicott notes the marred rhythm (a particle lost from Ps. 105:11) and glosses 'the lot' as literally 'the measuring line' (Ps. 16:5) — Canaan as a measured, apportioned domain.
Henry: the land of Canaan is the very substance of the everlasting covenant, sure to the fathers though they died strangers in it.

themes: a real inheritance, not an abstraction

schools.summary: Ellicott opens the fourth strophe of Ps. 105, notes 'ye' for the original 'they,' and reads 'few' as 'men of number' (Gen. 34:30) and 'strangers' as sojourners (Gen. 23:4).
Henry: the patriarchs were few, unsettled, and exposed among ill-willers, yet no man was suffered to wrong them.

themes: few and strangers in the land
covenant faith at its most exposed
the promise resting on God alone

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'and they went from nation to nation' confirms the third-person 'they' as original; the connecting word is prosaic and absent from Ps. 105:13 — a mark of the Chronicler's looser hand.
Henry: the fathers were continually upon the remove and so exposed, passing among nations that might easily have destroyed them.

themes: the Keeper who went with them

schools.summary: Ellicott: this verse is the apodosis to v.19 — 'he suffered no man' ('permitted to no man,' cf. 2 Sam. 16:11) — binding fewness, wandering, and protection into one continuous act of God.
Henry: no man — Canaanite, Philistine, or Egyptian — was suffered to wrong them, and kings were reproved and plagued for their sakes, as Pharaoh and Abimelech.

themes: he suffered no man to do them wrong

schools.summary: Clarke: 'mine anointed' are the patriarchs, with regal and sacerdotal power, for whom God interfered (Gen. 12:17; 20:3; 31:24; 34:26; 35:5), though the title may reach to all the elect people (Heb. 11:26).

Ellicott reads 'mine anointed' as a plural of Messiah (Abraham and Sarah progenitors of kings, Gen. 17:16), notes the unparalleled construction of 'my prophets,' and marks the 'first seam' where the ode leaves Ps. 105 for Ps. 96 — a mosaic (Delitzsch).
Henry: the patriarchs, anointed and prophets, are the apple of God's eye; whoever harms them does so at his peril.

themes: the first seam in the mosaic
from Israel's guarded past to the nations

schools.summary: Clarke reports the Targum's expansion — 'let the supreme angels be glad' — noting it renders 'the heavens' by the Greek word for angels written in Hebrew letters.
Henry: the verse voices God's universal monarchy, the fear and joy of all people; the LORD reigns and by his providence establishes the world, so that though moved it cannot be removed.

Ellicott: the liturgic formula that opens several later psalms (Ps. 106, 107, 118, 136; Jer. 33:11) rounds the ode off, concluding with the thanksgiving with which it began (v.8); Keil places it at the hinge before the final prayer and doxology.
Henry marks the verse as drawn from Ps. 136:1, whose refrain 'his mercy endureth for ever' is the standing burden of the sanctuary's song.
Gill: David penned the plea by a spirit of prophecy, foreseeing Israel's captivity among the heathen, though he records that 'some think these were added by Ezra.'

Wesley reads it as David's prophetic provision for the church's future dispersion, with the rule that in our praises we must pray for God's servants in distress; Clarke dwells on praying under the attribute we need — 'O God of our salvation.'

debates.question: Were the closing petitions David's own by prophecy, or added by a later hand?

sides.position: David wrote them by a spirit of prophecy, foreseeing Israel's captivity among the heathen
they were added later, some think by Ezra, when the dispersion was a present grief

themes: save us, gather us, deliver us

schools.summary: Henry: worship is the work of every day, not a solemn day now and then; ark and altar had been parted since Eli's time, yet before the ark, with no sacrifice, David's prayers rose as incense — 'so early did spiritual worship take place of ceremonial.'
Keil and Delitzsch render 'according to the matter of each day on its day' and flag a textual snarl in 'Obed-edom and their brethren,' where a name has dropped out.
Wesley: David appointed them their work and station; prayer and lifted hands stood in place of incense and evening sacrifice.
Gill: David left Asaph and his brethren for the continual service of the ark.

themes: worship as the work of every day

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch detail the sixty-eight brethren and the porters Obed-edom and Hosah, and ask why no priest is named over the ark's worship — accounting for it by the chapter's sole concern with the sacred music, Abiathar being the unnamed overseer.
Gill: the Levites, brethren of Asaph and Obed-edom, were left before the ark to minister.

themes: the unnamed overseer of the ark

schools.summary: Wesley makes Zadok chief priest at Gibeon (the Mosaic tabernacle and its ordinary sacrifices), Abiathar over the extraordinary worship before the ark; Clarke: two high priests and two services, yet only one ark, the chief functions at Jerusalem.
Gill notes only that David left Zadok and his fellow-priests, having appointed them.

themes: a transitional order until the temple

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch unfold the standing order — burnt offerings morning and evening, all that is written in the law — with Heman and Jeduthun and the chosen singers joined to give thanks that God's mercy endures for ever.
Wesley: the sacrifices, though inferior to prayer and praise, must be kept up as types of the mediation of Christ, of mighty importance.
Gill: to offer burnt offerings was the work of the priests only.

themes: all that is written in the law

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch identify the Gibeon singers — Heman, Jeduthun, and the rest 'expressed by name,' including those of the earlier lists not assigned to Asaph on Zion — all set to give thanks that God's mercy endures for ever.
Henry: the burden of all their songs must be, 'For his mercy endureth for ever' — the fittest matter of a ceaseless praise.
Gill: 'with them' means with Zadok and the priests at Gibeon.

Wesley: 'instruments of God' are appropriated to his worship — common mirth and holy joy differing vastly; but Clarke, surveying the versions and the Syriac, turns the verse into a sustained polemic against instrumental music in Christian worship.
Keil and Delitzsch read 'the instruments of God's song,' note the sons of Jeduthun as porters, and close the chapter's business with David returning to bless (not merely greet) his house.
Gill settles the grammar — the trumpets and cymbals were with Heman and Jeduthun (Kimchi), or 'left' with them (Piscator).

outliers.note: Turns the verse into a polemic against instrumental music in Christian worship — doubting that God ever ordained instruments in his worship, that Christ or the apostles sanctioned them, or that mere wordless sounds can be praise, and calling the argument from Jewish usage 'futile in the extreme when applied to Christianity.'

schools.summary: Ellicott: the verse duplicates 2 Sam. 6:19-20 ('departed' plural, 'returned' better 'went round'); with Keil he notes the Chronicler omits the Michal scene as purely domestic, and reads 'to bless his house' as a real benediction, not a greeting.
Gill: the people departed home, having accompanied the ark, praised the Lord, and been refreshed with food.

themes: David returns to bless his house
